name: kubernetes
context: Kubernetes container orchestration — deploy, scale, network, and manage containerized workloads in clusters
info: The Kubernetes ecosystem covers production container orchestration: deploying applications as Pods managed by Deployments, exposing them via Services and Ingress, storing configuration in ConfigMaps and Secrets, persisting data with Volumes, managing access with RBAC, running batch work with Jobs, and autoscaling with HPA. All operations use kubectl, the Kubernetes API, or Helm charts. This tree is organized around the resources an agent actually manages: Pods, Deployments, Services, Ingress, ConfigMaps, Namespaces, Volumes, RBAC, Jobs, and HPA. Kubernetes runs on every major cloud (EKS, GKE, AKS) and locally via minikube, kind, or k3d.
body:
# Kubernetes Ecosystem

Kubernetes (k8s) is the industry-standard container orchestration platform. Agents use it to deploy, scale, expose, configure, and debug containerized applications across clusters. Every major cloud provider offers managed Kubernetes (EKS, GKE, AKS), and local development uses minikube, kind, or k3d.

## Architecture

Kubernetes clusters have two planes:

| Component | Role |
|-----------|------|
| **Control Plane** | API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager — manages desired state |
| **Worker Nodes** | kubelet, kube-proxy, container runtime — runs workloads |
| **kubectl** | CLI client that talks to the API server |
| **Helm** | Package manager for templated resource bundles (charts) |

The API server is the single entry point. Every `kubectl` command translates to an HTTP request to the API server. The desired state (YAML manifests) is stored in etcd, and controllers reconcile actual state to match.

## Core Resources

Kubernetes resources form a hierarchy:

- **[[kubernetes/kubectl]]** — the CLI for all cluster operations: apply, get, describe, logs, exec, scale
- **[[kubernetes/config]]** — kubeconfig files, contexts, authentication, and cluster connection
- **[[kubernetes/pods]]** — smallest deployable unit; one or more containers sharing network/storage
- **[[kubernetes/deployments]]** — declarative updates for Pods; manages ReplicaSets, rollouts, and rollbacks
- **[[kubernetes/services]]** — stable networking: ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, ExternalName
- **[[kubernetes/ingress]]** — HTTP/HTTPS routing with TLS termination and path-based rules
- **[[kubernetes/configmaps]]** — externalized configuration and secrets for Pods
- **[[kubernetes/volumes]]** — persistent storage via PV, PVC, and StorageClasses
- **[[kubernetes/namespaces]]** — cluster multi-tenancy and resource isolation
- **[[kubernetes/rbac]]** — access control with Roles, ClusterRoles, and ServiceAccounts
- **[[kubernetes/jobs]]** — batch and scheduled workloads (Jobs, CronJobs)
- **[[kubernetes/hpa]]** — horizontal autoscaling based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics
- **[[kubernetes/helm]]** — package management for repeatable deployments
- **[[kubernetes/troubleshooting]]** — debug CrashLoopBackOff, OOMKilled, scheduling failures, networking issues

## Agent Jobs

The primary tasks an agent performs in this ecosystem:

- **Deploy an app** — write a Deployment + Service manifest, apply it, verify rollout. See [[kubernetes/deployments]].
- **Expose an app** — create a Service and/or Ingress to make it reachable. See [[kubernetes/services]], [[kubernetes/ingress]].
- **Configure an app** — inject env vars and files from ConfigMaps and Secrets. See [[kubernetes/configmaps]].
- **Scale an app** — manually scale replicas or set up HPA for auto-scaling. See [[kubernetes/hpa]].
- **Debug a failure** — inspect events, logs, describe resources, exec into pods. See [[kubernetes/troubleshooting]].
- **Install a chart** — deploy third-party software via Helm. See [[kubernetes/helm]].

## kubectl Basics

```bash
# Set context
kubectl config use-context my-cluster
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=my-app

# Apply manifests
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
kubectl apply -f service.yaml

# Get resources
kubectl get pods -n my-app
kubectl get deployments
kubectl get services
kubectl get events --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp

# Describe and debug
kubectl describe pod my-pod
kubectl logs my-pod -c my-container --tail=100
kubectl exec -it my-pod -- /bin/sh

# Delete resources
kubectl delete -f deployment.yaml
kubectl delete pod my-pod
```

## Manifest Structure

Every Kubernetes resource is defined as a YAML manifest:

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-app
  namespace: production
  labels:
    app: my-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-app
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: my-app
          image: myregistry/my-app:v1.2.0
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 100m
              memory: 128Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 512Mi
```

## Environment Setup

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|----------|
| `kubectl` | CLI to interact with the Kubernetes API |
| `helm` | Package manager for Kubernetes charts |
| `minikube` | Local single-node cluster for development |
| `kind` | Kubernetes-in-Docker for CI and testing |
| `k3d` | Lightweight k3s clusters in Docker |
| `kubectx` / `kubens` | Fast context and namespace switching |
| `stern` | Multi-pod log tailing |
| `k9s` | Terminal UI for cluster management |

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `KUBECONFIG` | Path to kubeconfig file (default: `~/.kube/config`) |
| `KUBE_CONTEXT` | Active cluster context name |
| `KUBE_NAMESPACE` | Default namespace for commands |

sections.title: Cluster architecture
sections.body: A Kubernetes cluster has a control plane (API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager) and worker nodes (kubelet, kube-proxy, container runtime). The API server is the single entry point — kubectl and all clients talk to it via HTTPS. etcd stores all cluster state. The scheduler assigns Pods to nodes. Controllers reconcile desired state (manifests in etcd) with actual state (running containers).

sections.title: Resource model
sections.body: Everything in Kubernetes is a resource defined by apiVersion, kind, metadata, and spec. Resources are namespaced (Pods, Deployments, Services) or cluster-scoped (Nodes, Namespaces, ClusterRoles). Resources are created, read, updated, and deleted via the API server. Labels and selectors connect resources — a [[kubernetes/services]] selects Pods by label, a [[kubernetes/deployments]] manages Pods via a label selector.

sections.title: kubectl command patterns
sections.body: Core verbs: `get`, `describe`, `apply`, `delete`, `logs`, `exec`, `port-forward`, `scale`, `rollout`. Use `-o yaml` or `-o json` for machine-readable output. Use `-n namespace` to target a namespace. Use `--all-namespaces` or `-A` to search everywhere. Use `-l app=my-app` to filter by label. Use `--watch` for real-time updates. Dry-run with `--dry-run=client -o yaml` to generate manifests without applying.
